lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market contract manufacturers and co-packers whose recurring customer work is the canning, pickling, brining, retort processing, filling, labeling, and related quality-control of fruit- and vegetable-based products.
lens.excluded_activities: Branded manufacturers that only sell their own goods, captive plants, distributors, brokers, non-control financing vehicles, and establishments whose primary activity falls outside NAICS 311421.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Brand owners, retailers, foodservice companies, and other food businesses purchase recurring production runs under private-label or co-manufacturing arrangements, usually priced per case, unit, or run with customer specifications and volume commitments.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupation employment and wages for the broader NAICS 311400 population, not task-level AI exposure or 311421 alone. The estimate maps greater exposure to planning, ordering, recordkeeping, customer service, quality documentation, visual inspection support, and management work while assigning much less exposure to line operation, maintenance, sanitation, and material movement. Broader-industry inclusion and the absence of task microdata can bias either direction; treating physical automation as separate from AI limits upward bias.
a.rationale: BLS reports that production occupations are 50.95% and transportation/material-moving occupations 17.08% of broader-sector employment, while office support is 5.73%; key jobs include food batchmakers, packaging-machine operators, inspectors, planners, and inventory clerks. AI can reduce documentation, scheduling, forecasting, customer-service, and inspection-review labor, but most plant-floor work remains embodied and process-specific.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory from 10-15 independent 311421 co-packers, paired with time-and-motion studies for quality, scheduling, maintenance, customer service, and production roles, would materially change the range.
caveats: OEWS is for NAICS 311400 rather than 311421 and covers employers of all sizes.
The estimate concerns current not-yet-automated tasks; installed physical automation and conventional control systems are not counted as new AI opportunity.

rho.bridge: Deloitte surveys manufacturers across industries and company sizes about smart-manufacturing adoption and outcomes, not five-year implementation of the specific AI-exposed task share at LMM canners. Its reported productivity gains and automation priorities establish feasibility, then the estimate is reduced for smaller-firm capital, data, integration, food-safety validation, and change constraints. The survey's large-company mix likely biases implementation upward for this lens.
rho.rationale: Manufacturers in Deloitte's survey reported 7%-20% employee-productivity improvements from smart-manufacturing initiatives and 46% ranked process automation as a top-two investment priority. Implementation at canners should be slower because product-specific scheduled processes, preventive controls, traceability, legacy equipment, seasonal throughput, and customer audits require controlled rollout and human accountability.
rho.change_evidence: Obtain system-level adoption, payback, exception-rate, and labor-hour results from ERP/MES, machine-vision, maintenance, and quality-documentation deployments at LMM fruit and vegetable co-packers, including failed pilots.
caveats: Smart manufacturing includes non-AI technologies, so reported outcomes do not isolate AI.
FDA requirements constrain implementation but do not prohibit decision support or administrative automation.

e.rationale: NAICS 311421 is defined by manufacturing products, not by an outsourced-service revenue model. A meaningful minority nevertheless operate as co-packers or private-label manufacturers producing recurring runs for brand owners; branded-only manufacturers do not qualify. The estimate allows mixed branded/contract plants only when the contract activity is operationally material and transferable.
e.change_evidence: Classify all 118 dataset-estimated LMM firms using customer concentration, private-label/co-pack revenue share, websites, SQF audit profiles, and customer contracts; count only firms with recurring external contract-manufacturing revenue.
caveats: No census source identifies contract-manufacturing revenue within 311421.
The provided firm count is an EBITDA-band estimate and may include branded manufacturers or firms with mixed NAICS activities.

s5.bridge: Gallup observed five-year sale/transfer intentions for U.S. employer-business owners across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers of eligible LMM 311421 firms. The 22% employer intention rate anchors the base; the range discounts failed intentions and excludes non-control events, while food-processing M&A activity supports that a functioning buyer market exists. Intentions likely overstate completions, while unplanned sales and strategic divestitures create offsetting understatement.
s5.rationale: Gallup found 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. Food-processing M&A rose from 20 deals in 2023 to 52 in 2024, demonstrating transfer activity, though those transactions span sizes and subsectors and are not a denominator-based rate.
s5.change_evidence: Build a five-year denominator-based transfer cohort for independent 311421 co-packers from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, BizBuySell, state filings, and broker interviews, distinguishing control deals from closures, recapitalizations, and internal reorganizations.
caveats: Owner plans are not completed transfers and the Gallup sample is cross-industry.
Published food M&A counts skew toward reportable and larger transactions.

q.rationale: Per-unit and per-run co-manufacturing contracts can let an operator retain internal labor savings during a contract term, but retailer and brand-owner bargaining, bid renewals, open-book costing, volume commitments, and competing co-packers should share a substantial portion over five years. Savings tied to better yield, uptime, compliance, and service reliability are likely more retainable than visible direct-labor reductions.
q.change_evidence: Review a stratified sample of customer contracts and renewal histories for pricing formula, cost-plus clauses, productivity givebacks, audit rights, term length, minimum volumes, and realized gross-margin changes after automation.
caveats: Contract structures and customer concentration vary widely by product and customer.
No public source measures five-year retained AI benefit for 311421 co-packers.

d5.bridge: USDA availability series measure national food-category supply available for consumption, not constant-price demand for the exact recurring contract-manufacturing basket. Processing-vegetable and canned-fruit trends are carried forward over five years, with a wider range for sauces, pickles, private label, foodservice, population growth, and mix. Availability can diverge from final demand because of trade, inventory, and waste, and the long historical decline may overstate near-term contraction.
d5.rationale: USDA reports processing-vegetable availability fell 32 pounds per person from 1996 to 2024 and that fresh pineapple's share rose from 17% in 1995-97 to two-thirds in 2024, evidencing substitution away from processed forms. Shelf-stable sauces, pickles, foodservice inputs, and value-oriented private label temper the decline.
d5.change_evidence: Use five years of Circana/Nielsen unit volumes for canned fruit, vegetables, tomato products, pickles, relishes, jams, and sauces plus customer shipment data from eligible co-packers, deflated and quality/mix adjusted.
caveats: Processing vegetables include frozen products outside 311421.
Aggregate per-capita series obscure population growth and divergent product trends.

o.bridge: FDA rules establish that regulated processors must register facilities, file scheduled processes, and maintain preventive-control oversight; they do not measure the future quantity share requiring an operator. The estimate treats physical transformation, plant accountability, and customer-specific quality obligations as strong evidence that software alone cannot supply the service. It may overstate operator retention if customers vertically integrate or shift to fresh/frozen substitutes.
o.rationale: Canning requires physical ingredients, thermal or acidification processes, filling, sealing, sanitation, testing, and accountable food-safety controls. FDA requires establishment registration and product/process filings for shelf-stable acidified and low-acid canned foods, and preventive-control rules require facilities to ensure controls are met. AI can change labor and decisions but cannot replace the accountable plant operator for surviving demand.
o.change_evidence: Interview brand-owner procurement leaders and 311421 operators about five-year insourcing, format substitution, lights-out production, regulatory responsibility, and whether software vendors could displace rather than augment the co-packer.
caveats: Regulation proves accountability requirements, not continued use of independent co-packers.
Demand eliminated by product-format substitution is accounted for in d5, not again here.

narrative.executive_view: The relevant opportunity is a minority of 311421 firms that repeatedly manufacture private-label or customer-formulated canned, pickled, brined, and sauce products. AI can improve planning, quality records, inspection triage, maintenance, inventory, and customer administration, but the industry's labor base is dominated by physical production and material movement. Demand faces a long-run shift toward fresh alternatives, while regulatory accountability makes the surviving service difficult to replace with software alone.
narrative.ai_impact: Near-term AI value is primarily decision support and workflow compression around production scheduling, procurement, demand forecasting, quality documentation, deviation investigation, customer service, and machine-vision review. Direct substitution on retort, filling, sanitation, maintenance, and material-handling work is limited unless paired with capital-intensive physical automation, which is outside the pure AI effect.
narrative.value_capture: Savings can remain with the operator during fixed-price contract periods and through better yield, uptime, fewer errors, and improved service. Retention erodes at rebids and renewals when sophisticated brand owners or retailers demand productivity sharing, especially under open-book or cost-plus arrangements. High customer concentration would make capture weaker.
narrative.firm_availability: Most firms in the NAICS code are product manufacturers rather than outsourced-service providers, so eligibility is the first major filter. Transfer evidence is cross-industry, but employer-owner intentions and active food-processing deal flow suggest that control transfers occur often enough to warrant direct market mapping.
narrative.demand_durability: Canned and processed fruit and vegetable categories face substitution toward fresh and other formats, producing a likely modest five-year decline in the current basket. Yet shelf stability, affordability, foodservice use, sauces, pickled products, and private label preserve a substantial base. Any surviving volume still requires a compliant physical processor.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the true share of contract-manufacturing firms inside 311421, customer-contract economics, product mix, and the lack of task-level labor studies for smaller canners. Food-safety failures, recalls, raw-material volatility, steel/container cost, seasonal capacity, retailer concentration, and customer insourcing can overwhelm administrative productivity gains.
narrative.principal_driver: Regulated, recurring physical production makes the surviving co-manufacturing relationship operationally sticky and operator-required.
narrative.principal_weakness: Only a minority of the NAICS population fits the outsourced-service lens, and core canned-product demand is under secular substitution pressure.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: 311421 Fruit and Vegetable Canning
sources.supports: Defines 311421 as establishments manufacturing canned, pickled, and brined fruits and vegetables and lists representative products and exclusions.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 311400
sources.supports: Reports employment and wages by occupation for the broader preserving and specialty-food sector, including 50.95% production, 17.08% transportation/material moving, 9.18% maintenance, and 5.73% office-support employment shares.

sources.title: 2025 Smart Manufacturing and Operations Survey
sources.supports: Reports a 600-respondent manufacturing survey, including 7%-20% employee-productivity improvement, 10%-20% output improvement, and 46% ranking process automation as a top-two investment priority.

sources.title: Acidified and Low-Acid Canned Foods Guidance Documents and Regulatory Information
sources.supports: States that commercial processors of shelf-stable acidified and low-acid canned foods must register establishments and file scheduled processes for each product, style, container, and method.

sources.title: FSMA Final Rule for Preventive Controls for Human Food
sources.supports: Describes risk-based preventive-control, training, oversight, and management requirements for covered food facilities.

sources.title: Contract Manufacturing of Food Products
sources.supports: Explains the co-packing/private-label relationship, customer recipe and specification transfer, recurring manufacturing steps, quality control, labeling, and regulatory compliance.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership in the next five years, based on a fall 2024 survey of 1,264 U.S. business owners.

sources.title: Food Production Mergers and Acquisitions Update
sources.supports: Reports 144 food-production transactions in 2024 and an increase in food-processing deals from 20 in 2023 to 52 in 2024.

sources.title: Vegetable Availability Declined in 2024
sources.supports: Reports U.S. vegetable and pulse availability of 376 pounds per person in 2024 and a 32-pound decline in processing vegetables since 1996.

sources.title: Pineapple Availability Pivots from Processed to Fresh
sources.supports: Reports fresh pineapple rising from about 17% of availability in 1995-97 to two-thirds in 2024, evidencing substitution away from canned and juice forms.
